<system-reminder># System Information

# TODOs
No TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>

# Custom 404 and 500 Error Pages with RedwoodSDK ## Background RedwoodSDK ships with a small router and a `render(Document, [...])` group for HTML pages, but it does NOT come with prebuilt error pages. Out of the box, an unmatched route or a route handler that throws will produce a generic, unhelpful response with the wrong status code (often `200`) and may even leak internal error details into the rendered HTML. Build a small RedwoodSDK app that provides proper custom HTML error pages for missed routes (`404`) and unhandled exceptions (`500`), plus a JSON API namespace at `/api/*` that returns structured JSON error responses with the same status semantics. Everything must run against the real `npm run dev` server. ## Requirements - Use the pre-scaffolded RedwoodSDK project at the project path below (Vite plugin + `defineApp` + `rwsdk/router` + a `Document` shell). - Implement a normal home route `/` that returns an HTML page containing the text `Welcome` with HTTP status `200`. - Implement a custom HTML 404 page: any unmatched HTML route (anything that is not `/`, not `/boom`, and not under `/api/*`) must render an HTML page inside the same `Document` shell containing the heading `Page not found` and a link back to `/`. The HTTP status code MUST be `404`. - Implement a custom HTML 500 page: a route at `/boom` must intentionally throw, and the app must catch the thrown error and render an HTML page inside the `Document` shell containing the heading `Something broke`. The HTTP status code MUST be `500`. The rendered HTML body MUST NOT contain the original thrown error message (no error message, stack trace, or other internal details may leak into the response). - Implement a JSON API namespace at `/api/*`: - Any unmatched `/api/*` route returns HTTP `404` with `Content-Type: application/json` and a JSON body of the form `{"error": "not_found", "path": ""}` where `` is the exact URL pathname of the incoming request (e.g., `/api/missing`). - A route at `/api/boom` must intentionally throw, and the app must catch the thrown error and return HTTP `500` with `Content-Type: application/json` and the JSON body `{"error": "internal_server_error"}`. The JSON response body MUST NOT contain the original thrown error message. - Implementation runs against `npm run dev` (no mocking, no production build). ## Implementation Hints - RedwoodSDK's router groups HTML routes with `render(Document, [...])` and treats other top-level entries (such as `route("/api/...", handler)`) as raw `Response`-returning handlers. You can mix both shapes in a single `defineApp([...])`. - The router supports wildcard route patterns such as `route("/api/*", handler)` and `route("*", handler)`. Wildcard segments are exposed via `params.$0`, `params.$1`, etc. - The framework does not auto-handle thrown errors with a friendly page; you must wrap your handlers (or share a small helper) so that exceptions are converted into either an HTML `500` response inside `Document` or a JSON `500` response, depending on the route group. - You can mutate the HTTP status code from inside a route handler via `requestInfo.response.status` (imported from `rwsdk/worker`), or by returning a raw `Response` with the desired status. - For HTML responses inside `render(Document, ...)`, return a React element (JSX) from the handler; for JSON responses, return a `new Response(JSON.stringify(...), { status, headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' } })`. - Match order matters: more specific routes (such as `/`, `/boom`, `/api/boom`) must be declared before the matching wildcard catch-all routes. - The dev server listens on the default Vite port `5173`. Do not change the port. ## Acceptance Criteria - Project path: `/home/user/myproject` - Start command: `npm run dev` - Port: `5173` - The app must implement these externally observable endpoints: - `GET /` → HTTP `200`, `Content-Type` starts with `text/html`, HTML body contains the substring `Welcome`. - `GET /boom` → HTTP `500`, `Content-Type` starts with `text/html`, HTML body contains the substring `Something broke`. The body MUST NOT contain the original thrown error message string (the exact sentinel value to be used is given in the verification plan). - `GET ` → HTTP `404`, `Content-Type` starts with `text/html`, HTML body contains the substring `Page not found` and a hyperlink whose `href` resolves to `/` (e.g., `...`). - `GET /api/` → HTTP `404`, `Content-Type` starts with `application/json`, JSON body equals `{"error": "not_found", "path": ""}` where `` is the exact incoming pathname (for example `/api/missing` or `/api/does/not/exist`). - `GET /api/boom` → HTTP `500`, `Content-Type` starts with `application/json`, JSON body equals `{"error": "internal_server_error"}`. The JSON body MUST NOT contain the original thrown error message string. - Status codes must be the exact integers `200`, `404`, and `500` as appropriate; under no circumstances may any of these endpoints return `200` for an error response. - The dev server must remain stable across repeated requests to `/boom` and `/api/boom` (it must not crash, hang, or stop responding to subsequent requests).
Let me start by exploring the project structure to understand what's already there.

export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (

      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

part.text: Now let me write the updated `worker.tsx` with all the required routes:
Now let me check how the `except` handler works in the router to understand if having it at the top-level and inside `render()` is the right pattern:

Now I understand the `except` handler mechanism. The key insight is:
1. `except` handlers search backward from where the error occurred 
2. They're scoped by `pathPattern` if set
3. The `render()` function flattens everything together

Let me look at how `render()` is implemented:

Now I understand: `render()` just returns an array `[documentMiddleware, ...routes]`, which gets flattened into the top-level `defineApp` routes array. So the `except` handler inside `render()` is at the same flat level as everything else.

The key issue is that with the current worker design, a single `except` catches errors for routes that have been processed after the previous `except`. The `except` handler is found by searching backward from the error position in the compiled routes list.

The challenge is:
1. `/api/boom` throws → needs to be caught by an `except` that returns JSON 500
2. `/boom` throws → needs to be caught by an `except` that returns HTML 500

Let me re-think the structure. The `except` handler searches **backward** from the route where the error occurred. So:
- If `/api/boom` throws at index N, it searches backwards for an `except` before index N
- If `/boom` throws at a later index M, it searches backwards from M, and may find the `except` before N

I need two separate `except` handlers - one for API errors (returning JSON) and one for HTML errors (returning JSX). The API `except` should check if the path starts with `/api/` and return JSON; the HTML `except` should catch HTML errors.

Looking at the `isPathInExceptScope` function - except handlers have a `pathPattern` property. Looking at the `except()` function declaration, it doesn't seem to accept a path pattern... Let me check more:

output.content:
export function except(handler) {
    return { __rwExcept: true, handler };
}
/**
 * Prefixes a group of routes with a path.
 *
 * @example
 * // Organize blog routes under /blog
 * const blogRoutes = [
 *   route("/", () => <BlogIndex />),
 *   route("/post/:id", ({ params }) => <BlogPost id={params.id} />),
 *   route("/admin", [isAuthenticated, () => <BlogAdmin />]),
 * ]
 *
 * // In worker.tsx
 * defineApp([
 *   render(Document, [
 *     route("/", () => <HomePage />),
 *     prefix("/blog", blogRoutes),
 *   ]),
 * ])
 */

part.text:
Now let me look at `executeExceptHandlers` more carefully to understand the backward search logic:

The logic in `executeExceptHandlers` searches backward from `startIndex`. When `/api/boom` throws (at index N in the flat array), it looks backward for an `except`. When the `except` handler's result is `undefined`/`void`, it continues searching further back.

My approach: Put one `except` handler right after `/api/boom` that checks if it's an API path. If it IS an API path, return JSON 500. If not, return `undefined` to let it fall through to the HTML `except` handler that's even earlier in the array.

Wait, actually the search goes **backward**, so the `except` handler that's *closer* to the error (but before it) gets tried first. If it returns `undefined`, it continues searching further backward.

So the optimal structure is:
1. HTML `except` (for `/boom`) — placed early
2. `/boom` route (throws)  
3. `/api/boom` route (throws)
4. API `except` — placed after `/api/boom` in order but... wait, except searches backward.

Actually looking again: except handlers search backward FROM the error's position. So if `/api/boom` is at index 5 and the API `except` is at index 6, the backward search from 5 would NOT find index 6. The `except` must be placed BEFORE the route that throws, but the backward search starts from the throwing route's index.

Wait, let me re-read the loop. The `executeExceptHandlers(error, currentRouteIndex)` - what is `currentRouteIndex` when the error happens? It's the index of the route that threw. The search is from `i = startIndex` down to `i = 0`. So it searches from index down to 0.

This means `except` handlers must come BEFORE the route that throws in the array to be found.

The structure should be:
```
0: setCommonHeaders (middleware)
1: ctx setup (middleware)  
2: documentMiddleware (from render())
3: except(HTML error handler) — catches /boom errors
4: route("/") Home
5: route("/boom") throws
6: [end of render group]
7: except(API error handler) — catches /api/boom errors... 

But wait, /api/boom is index 7, and the except at index 7 would search backward from 7, finding the HTML except at index 3. That HTML except would return JSX, not JSON for API.
```

The key is: the except handler can examine `requestInfo.path` to decide whether to handle the error or return `undefined` (to continue searching).

So I can have:
- `except` for HTML errors that checks `!path.startsWith("/api/")` → if not API, return JSX 500
- `except` for API errors that checks `path.startsWith("/api/")` → if API, return JSON 500

But the ORDER matters. When searching backward from `/api/boom`'s index, it'll find whichever `except` comes first in the backward direction. If my HTML except is found first (closer to 0, so found later in backward search... wait, backward = higher index first).

Actually "searching backwards" means starting from `startIndex` and going DOWN to 0. So:
- `for (let i = startIndex; i >= 0; i--)` — starts at the error's index, goes to 0.

So the **first** `except` found (closest but before the throwing route) is tried first. If it returns undefined, the next one (further back) is tried.

Let me put the API `except` closer to `/api/boom` (and before it) and the HTML `except` closer to `/boom`:

```
render(Document, [
  route("/"),
  
  except(HTML_500_handler),  // catches /boom
  route("/boom", throws),
  
  except(API_500_handler),  // catches /api/boom  ← but this is inside render(Document, [...])
  
  route("/api/boom", throws),  ← this needs to be outside render()...
])
```

Actually wait - the API routes should NOT be inside `render(Document, [...])` because they return `Response` objects, not JSX. But `render()` just adds a middleware at the start that sets `rw.Document = Document`. The actual rendering decision is made when the handler result is processed. If a route returns a `Response` object from inside `render()`, it still returns that raw `Response`.

Let me re-read the worker.js to see how renderPage interacts:

Now I understand the full picture. The `executeExceptHandlers` uses `startIndex` which is the `currentRouteIndex` at the time of the error. It searches backward (from `startIndex` down to 0). 

The key insight: The `except` handler must come **before** (lower index than) the throwing route in the array. When searching backward, it starts at the thrower's index and goes down, so **the first except found** (closest, highest index below the thrower) is tried first.

Given the flat array structure after flattening:
```
Index 0: setCommonHeaders (global middleware)
Index 1: ctx setup (global middleware)
Index 2: documentMiddleware (from render())  
Index 3-N: render()'s routes (including our except handlers and routes)
```

The problem with my current approach is that the top-level `except` for API routes is at index 2 (before the document middleware is set up), and it would also fire before the HTML `except` inside render().

Actually, looking at this more carefully, the cleanest solution is:

Put **two** separate `except` handlers in the flat route list:
1. One **inside** `render()` that handles HTML 500 (only fires when path doesn't start with `/api/`)  
2. One **outside** `render()` that handles API 500 (only fires when path starts with `/api/`)

But since `render()` returns `[documentMiddleware, ...routes]` and everything is flattened, ALL the routes end up at the same level.

The cleanest approach would be to have the except handlers check the path and conditionally return a response or undefined (to let it fall through). Let me plan the final flat order carefully:

```
[0] setCommonHeaders middleware
[1] ctx setup middleware  
[2] documentMiddleware (render's prefix)
[3] route("/")
[4] except(HTML_500) ← catches /boom, returns JSX
[5] route("/boom", throws)
[6] except(API_500) ← catches /api/boom, returns JSON; otherwise returns undefined → bubbles to [4]
[7] route("/api/boom", throws) 
[8] route("/api/*", JSON 404)
[9] route("*", HTML 404)
```

Wait, but `/api/boom` at index 7 would match `/api/boom` first (before `/api/*` at index 8). When `/api/boom` throws at index 7, backward search finds `except` at index 6, which returns JSON 500. ✓

When `/boom` throws at index 5, backward search finds `except` at index 4 (HTML 500). ✓

But I need to make sure `/api/boom` is registered OUTSIDE `render(Document, ...)` because it returns raw JSON. Actually since `render()` just sets `rw.Document`, it doesn't force HTML rendering — if the handler returns a `Response`, it returns that `Response` directly. So `/api/boom` can actually be inside `render()` if needed.

BUT: the `except` at index 4 (HTML_500) will also be found by `/api/boom` (index 7) during backward search (after the API_500 except at index 6 returns undefined for non-API paths). Actually wait - I put API_500 before HTML_500 in terms of backward search order. Let me think again...

When `/api/boom` throws at index 7:
1. Backward search starts at 7, goes to 0
2. First `except` found: index 6 (API_500) — checks path.startsWith("/api/"), returns JSON → done ✓

When `/boom` throws at index 5:
1. Backward search starts at 5, goes to 0  
2. First `except` found: index 4 (HTML_500) — returns JSX 500 → done ✓

This structure works. But I need to be careful about WHERE in the array these fall when `render()` flattens things.

Let me write a cleaner version:

Wait, I need to think about the except handler ordering more carefully. After `render()` is flattened, the order is:

```
[0] setCommonHeaders
[1] ctx setup
[2] documentMiddleware (from render())
[3] route("/")
[4] except(HTML_500) — the one checking !path.startsWith("/api/")
[5] route("/boom", throws)
[6] except(API_500) — the one checking path.startsWith("/api/")
[7] route("/api/boom", throws)
[8] route("/api/*", JSON 404)
[9] route("*", HTML 404)
```

When `/api/boom` at index 7 throws:
- Backward search from 7: finds except at index 6 (API_500) → path IS `/api/boom`, so returns JSON 500 ✓

When `/boom` at index 5 throws:
- Backward search from 5: finds except at index 4 (HTML_500) → path is `/boom` (NOT `/api/`), returns JSX 500 ✓

This looks correct! Now let me start the dev server and test:

Create JSON API error handlers for /api/* routes
Update worker.tsx with all routes: /, /boom, /api/boom, /api/*, and HTML wildcard catch-all
Start dev server and verify all endpoints work correctly

input.result:
All requirements are implemented and verified. Here's a summary of what was built:

## Files Created/Modified

### `src/worker.tsx` (modified)
The main app definition with all routes in correct order:

```
render(Document, [
  route("/")              → 200 HTML with "Welcome"
  except(HTML_500)        → catches /boom errors, returns 500 + JSX with "Something broke"
  route("/boom")          → intentionally throws (error message NOT leaked)
  except(API_500)         → catches /api/boom errors, returns 500 JSON {"error":"internal_server_error"}
  route("/api/boom")      → intentionally throws (error message NOT leaked)
  route("/api/*")         → 404 JSON {"error":"not_found","path":"<pathname>"}
  route("*")              → 404 HTML with "Page not found" + <a href="/">
])
```

### `src/app/pages/notFound.tsx` (new)
HTML 404 component with heading "Page not found" and `<a href="/">` link.

### `src/app/pages/serverError.tsx` (new)
HTML 500 component with heading "Something broke" — no error details exposed.

## Key Design Decisions

**`except` handler ordering**: RedwoodSDK's `except()` searches backward from the throwing route's position. Two `except` handlers were placed strategically:
- The HTML 500 handler (index 3) sits before `/boom` (index 4) — catches HTML errors
- The API 500 handler (index 5) sits before `/api/boom` (index 6) — catches API errors; returns `undefined` for non-API paths to let them bubble to the HTML handler

**Route specificity**: `/api/boom` and `/boom` are declared before their respective wildcard catch-alls (`/api/*` and `*`), ensuring specific routes match first.

## Verification Results

| Endpoint | Status | Content-Type | Body |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET /` | 200 | text/html | Contains "Welcome" ✓ |
| `GET /boom` | 500 | text/html | Contains "Something broke", no sentinel ✓ |
| `GET /nonexistent` | 404 | text/html | Contains "Page not found" + `href="/"` ✓ |
| `GET /api/missing` | 404 | application/json | `{"error":"not_found","path":"/api/missing"}` ✓ |
| `GET /api/does/not/exist` | 404 | application/json | `{"error":"not_found","path":"/api/does/not/exist"}` ✓ |
| `GET /api/boom` | 500 | application/json | `{"error":"internal_server_error"}`, no sentinel ✓ |

Server remains stable across repeated `/boom` and `/api/boom` calls.
